meta.title: Z3 2.8/3.0i Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: BMW Z3 2.8 & 3.0i engines — M52 and M54. Real UK costs, cooling system & VANOS insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW Z3 2.8 or 3.0i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Z3 engine costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only depending on which engine you have. The M52B28 (2.8) starts around £3,000; the M54B30 (3.0i) starts around £3,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW Z3 2.8 or 3.0i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both the M52 and M54 are widely considered some of BMW's most reliable engines — smooth, torquey, and capable of high mileages. A well-maintained Z3 can easily surpass 250,000 miles. However, they have known weak points: cooling system failures (plastic components), VANOS seal degradation, and oil leaks from the valve cover and oil filter housing gaskets. These are all well-understood and fixable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW Z3 2.8 and 3.0i?

acceptedAnswer.text: 2.8 (M52): Cooling system failures, single VANOS seal degradation, oil leaks, DISA valve wear (M52TU), and potential Nikasil lining issues on early European cars. 3.0i (M54): Cooling system failures, double VANOS seal degradation, oil leaks, DISA valve wear, and oil pump nut issues. Both engines share the Z3's rear subframe mounting weakness.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW Z3 2.8 or 3.0i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £8,000 Z3 3.0i, a £5,000 recon is 63% — approaching our 70% BMW threshold. Against a tired £4,000 example, the same recon exceeds the threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained Z3s are appreciating — a proper restoration is often justified.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW Z3 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. 2.8 (1997–2000): M52B28. 3.0i (2000–2002): M54B30. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 2.8 or 3.0i.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the cooling system on the Z3, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — cooling system repairs are well-understood and relatively affordable. A full cooling system overhaul (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank) costs £400–£900. If you catch overheating early, you can prevent head gasket failure. If the engine has already overheated severely, a head gasket or engine replacement may be needed. Preventative replacement of the cooling system is recommended at 60,000-mile intervals.

rows.bestFor: No longer available from BMW — these engines are out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the varying costs across the two engines. The M52B28 (2.8) is generally cheaper to replace; the M54B30 (3.0) commands a premium. A full engine replacement can cost between £1,500 and £5,000 on average. Reconditioned engines typically cost £1,200–£2,200 supply only.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Z3 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare Z3 2.8/3.0i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (M52 & M54)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, temperature gauge fluctuations, sweet smell from the engine bay, radiator or expansion tank cracks.
problems.repairCost: £400–£900 (cooling system overhaul — water pump, thermostat, expansion tank)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£7,500 (if overheating leads to head gasket or engine failure)
problems.recommendation: The M52 and M54's cooling systems are their Achilles heel. Plastic radiator end tanks, expansion tanks, and water pump impellers degrade over time. If you're experiencing coolant loss or overheating, have the entire cooling system inspected. Preventative replacement of the water pump, thermostat, and expansion tank is recommended at 60,000-mile intervals.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system failure guide

problems.title: VANOS Seal Degradation (M52 & M54)
problems.symptoms: Loss of low-end power, hesitation on acceleration, rough idle, poor fuel economy, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £200–£500 (VANOS seal replacement/rebuild)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: Both the M52's single VANOS and the M54's double VANOS use rubber seals that degrade over time, allowing oil pressure to escape. The fix is a VANOS seal replacement or rebuild using upgraded Viton seals — a well-understood, relatively affordable repair.

failureLink.label: Read the full VANOS failure guide

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing (M52 & M54)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the car, burning oil smell from the engine bay, low oil level warning.
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket are common on both engines. Later M52 engines with plastic valve covers are prone to cracking. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected promptly. Left unchecked, oil loss can lead to low oil levels and potential engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full oil leak guide

rows.whenItMakesSense: Affordable upgrade with Viton seals — far cheaper than engine replacement
Standard maintenance on ageing BMW engines
Specialist welding repair; far cheaper than a new car, but requires careful assessment

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Z3? Check These First
buyIf: * Cooling system overhaul completed (water pump, thermostat, expansion tank)
* VANOS seals replaced or upgraded to Viton
* Full service history with evidence of regular oil changes
* No evidence of rear subframe mounting issues
* Well-maintained Z3s can easily surpass 250,000 miles
avoidIf: * Coolant loss or overheating with no documented cooling system work
* Loss of low-end power or hesitation with no VANOS history
* Poor service history — these engines are reliable but need good maintenance
* Evidence of rear subframe tears or poor welding repairs
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Z3 2.8 and 3.0i represent the pinnacle of the standard Z3 range — powered by two of BMW's most celebrated naturally aspirated straight-six engines. The M52 and M54 are smooth, torquey, and reliable when properly maintained. But they're now ageing engines with known weak points: cooling systems, VANOS seals, oil leaks, and the Z3-specific rear subframe mounting issue. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained Z3 2.8 or 3.0i is a £5,000–£15,000 future classic; a neglected one is a £5,000 repair bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare Z3 2.8/3.0i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW Z3 2.8 or 3.0i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling, hesitation, or rough idle (VANOS or cooling system warning)
* Service History — including which engine (2.8 M52 or 3.0i M54) and confirmed oil-change intervals
* Coolant Check — Any history of coolant loss or cooling system work? (Achilles heel on both engines)
* VANOS Check — Any loss of low-end power, hesitation, or rough idle? (VANOS seals warning)
* Oil Leak Check — Any evidence of oil leaks around the valve cover or oil filter housing?
* Rear Subframe Check — Any evidence of subframe mounting tears or poor repairs?
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved VANOS, cooling system, or sensor issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (oxygen sensors are common)
